abstract: World action models (WAMs) have emerged as a promising direction for robot policy learning, as they can leverage powerful video backbones to model the future states. However, existing approaches often rely on separate action modules, or use action representations that are not pixel-grounded, making it difficult to fully exploit the pretrained knowledge of video models and limiting transfer across viewpoints and environments. In this work, we present Action Images, a unified world action model that formulates policy learning as multiview video generation. Instead of encoding control as low-dimensional tokens, we translate 7-DoF robot actions into interpretable action images: multi-view action videos that are grounded in 2D pixels and explicitly track robot-arm motion. This pixel-grounded action representation allows the video backbone itself to act as a zero-shot policy, without a separate policy head or action module. Beyond control, the same unified model supports video-action joint generation, action-conditioned video generation, and action labeling under a shared representation. On RLBench and real-world evaluations, our model achieves the strongest zero-shot success rates and improves video-action joint generation quality over prior video-space world models, suggesting that interpretable action images are a promising route to policy learning.

citations.fullCitation: Zhen, H., Sun, Q., Zhang, H., Li, J., Zhou, S., Du, Y., Gan, C.: Tesseract: learning 4d embodied world models. arXiv preprint arXiv:2504.20995 (2025)
citations.justification: This paper is a direct world-model-based baseline that 'Action Images' is compared against in its experimental evaluations. The authors of the main paper also worked on Tesseract, suggesting it is a closely related work whose limitations likely motivated the development of the pixel-grounded action representation.
citations.title: Tesseract: learning 4d embodied world models

citations.fullCitation: Kim, M.J., Gao, Y., Lin, T.Y., Lin, Y.C., Ge, Y., Lam, G., Liang, P., Song, S., Liu, M.Y., Finn, C., et al.: Cosmos policy: Fine-tuning video models for visuomotor control and planning. arXiv preprint arXiv:2601.16163 (2026)
citations.justification: This is a key competitive baseline used throughout the experiments. The main paper cites Cosmos-Policy as an example of an approach that uses action representations which are not spatially grounded, a core problem that 'Action Images' aims to solve by grounding actions directly in the pixel space.
citations.title: Cosmos policy: Fine-tuning video models for visuomotor control and planning

citations.fullCitation: Ye, S., Ge, Y., Zheng, K., Gao, S., Yu, S., Kurian, G., Indupuru, S., Tan, Y.L., Zhu, C., Xiang, J., et al.: World action models are zero-shot policies. arXiv preprint arXiv:2602.15922 (2026)
citations.justification: This paper, referred to as DreamZero in the text, is highlighted as a model with strong zero-shot generalization. However, 'Action Images' critiques its reliance on separate action modules, which motivates its own approach of creating a unified video backbone that acts as a zero-shot policy without needing an extra module.
citations.title: World action models are zero-shot policies

citations.fullCitation: Li, S., Gao, Y., Sadigh, D., Song, S.: Unified video action model. arXiv preprint arXiv:2503.00200 (2025)
citations.justification: This citation is presented as a key prior work that explores joint video-action generation. The 'Action Images' paper builds on this direction but differentiates itself by proposing a pixel-grounded action representation to create a more tightly integrated model, addressing the limitations of using separate action modules.
citations.title: Unified video action model

intermediateReport:
## Research Report: Action Images: End-to-End Policy Learning via Multiview Video Generation

### 1. Authors and Institution(s)

The research was conducted by a team of authors affiliated with several institutions:
*   Haoyu Zhen (UMass Amherst)
*   Zixian Gao (UTokyo)
*   Qiao Sun (UMass Amherst)
*   Yilin Zhao (NVIDIA)
*   Yuncong Yang (UMass Amherst)
*   Yilun Du (Harvard University)
*   Tsun-Hsuan Wang (Genesis AI)
*   Yi-Ling Qiao (Genesis AI)
*   Chuang Gan (UMass Amherst)

Haoyu Zhen and Zixian Gao are noted as equal contributors. Qiao Sun's contribution was made during an internship at UMass Amherst.

### 2. How This Work Fits into the Broader Research Landscape

This work is positioned within the field of robotics world models (WAMs) and generalist robot policy learning, aiming to bridge a gap between video generation capabilities and policy generalization. World action models have shown promise in predicting future observations by leveraging powerful video backbones. However, existing approaches frequently encounter challenges in translating this predictive ability into robust policy generalization, particularly in unseen environments.

Current methodologies for robot policy learning within world models often involve two main strategies:
1.  **Separate Action Modules:** Many methods attach a distinct policy head or action module to a world model to decode control from learned video features. This approach can limit the full exploitation of pretrained video model knowledge, as the burden of generalization is shifted to a specialized control component, potentially hindering transfer across different viewpoints or embodiments.
2.  **Non-Pixel-Grounded Action Representations:** Other approaches adapt video models for action generation but use representations that lack direct spatial grounding in the image space. This can lead to an indirect connection between the model's predictive world knowledge and its ability to act, making policy generalization difficult.

Concurrently, the development of generalist robot policy models has focused on creating control policies capable of diverse tasks and adaptable to new environments. While advances in Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models, Diffusion Policy, and Reinforcement Learning have improved generalizability, zero-shot transfer to entirely new environments often remains a challenge. Similarly, "4D" (3D plus time) generation models have advanced in creating dynamic scenes, but their application to robotic tasks has typically been limited to fixed single views or struggles with generalization beyond training scenes.

"Action Images" addresses these limitations by proposing a unified framework that reformulates policy learning as multiview video generation. It aims to integrate action directly into the video space through a pixel-grounded representation, thereby enabling the video backbone itself to function as a zero-shot policy. This approach distinguishes itself from prior work by seeking to more fully leverage pretrained video knowledge by making action an intrinsic part of the visual prediction problem, rather than relying on external action modules or abstract representations.

### 3. Key Objectives and Motivation

The primary objective of this research is to develop a unified robotics world action model that can effectively translate strong video generalization into robust policy generalization, particularly for zero-shot tasks. The authors identify a central limitation in current robotics world models: the discrepancy between a model's ability to successfully synthesize plausible future frames (video generalization) and its capacity to decide appropriate actions in novel or unseen environments (policy generalization).

This gap is attributed to the way actions are typically represented and integrated into world models:
*   **Detachment from Visual Space:** Existing action representations are often either low-dimensional tokens or latent codes that are not spatially grounded in the image space. This prevents the rich, pretrained knowledge embedded in large video backbones from being fully leveraged for action reasoning.
*   **Reliance on Separate Control Modules:** Many models delegate action decoding to a distinct policy head or action module. This setup shifts the responsibility for generalization to a specialized component, which often becomes the bottleneck for transferability across viewpoints, environments, or robot embodiments.

Motivated by these challenges, the key objectives of the "Action Images" framework are:

1.  **Formulate Policy Learning as Video Generation:** To treat robot control as a pixel-grounded visual prediction problem, rather than a separate, abstract task.
2.  **Develop a Pixel-Grounded Action Representation:** To translate 7-Degrees-of-Freedom (7-DoF) robot actions into interpretable multi-view action images that explicitly track robot-arm motion in image space. This representation is intended to align action with the robot's RGB observations, making it "native" to the video model.
3.  **Enable a Zero-Shot Policy without Separate Modules:** To allow the unified video backbone itself to serve as a zero-shot policy, directly generating actions as visual signals without the need for an additional policy head or action module. This aims to fully exploit the generalization capabilities inherent in video models.
4.  **Support Multiple Tasks within a Unified Model:** To demonstrate that the same video-space representation of observation and action can support diverse tasks, including video-action joint generation, action-conditioned video generation, and video-to-action labeling, under a single model and training objective.
5.  **Improve Generalizability and Transferability:** By grounding action in pixels and utilizing multi-view information, the research aims to achieve a policy model that generalizes more effectively across different viewpoints, motion patterns, and robot embodiments. Multi-view representation is specifically motivated to resolve 3D ambiguities inherent in single 2D projections.

In essence, the motivation is to simplify and unify robot learning by integrating action and observation into a common visual modality, thereby leveraging the power of large video generation models more directly for robotic control.

### 4. Methodology and Approach

The methodology of "Action Images" centers on representing robot actions as pixel-grounded images, integrating them into a unified video-space alongside observations, and training a large pre-trained video generator to model this combined data.

#### 4.1. Action as Images

The core idea involves converting a 7-DoF robot action into a visual representation.
*   **7-DoF Action to Semantic 3D Points:** Each robot action `a_t = [p_t, θ_t, g_t]` (end-effector position `p_t`, orientation `θ_t`, gripper openness `g_t`) is translated into three semantic 3D points:
    *   **Position Point:** `q_pos_t = p_t` (the end-effector position).
    *   **Up Point:** `q_up_t = p_t + ℓ R(θ_t)e_x` (following a canonical in-plane direction of the gripper).
    *   **Normal Point:** `q_normal_t = p_t + ℓ R(θ_t)(-e_z)` (normal to the robot gripper plane).
    `R(θ_t)` is the rotation matrix from the action orientation, and `ℓ` is a small length. These points aim to capture the end-effector pose.
*   **Multi-view Action Image Rendering:** The three semantic 3D points are projected into image space for each camera view `v` using camera intrinsics and extrinsics. These projected 2D points are then rendered into an action image `A^(v)_t ∈ R^(H×W×3)` as 2D Gaussian heatmaps:
    *   **Red Channel:** Encodes the position point.
    *   **Green Channel:** Encodes the normal point.
    *   **Blue Channel:** Encodes the up point as a Gaussian map, with binary gripper openness (`g_t`) injected into low-response background regions (pixels below a threshold of 0.25). This allows for encoding both a semantic point and a binary state in one channel.
*   **Action Videos:** Stacking these action images over time for each view creates an action video `A^(v)`. This yields a unified video-space representation where action videos share the same spatial and temporal structure as robot RGB observation videos `O^(v)`.

#### 4.2. Action Images Decoding

To use the generated action images for control, a decoding method is designed to convert them back into 7-DoF robot actions.
*   **Decoding Gripper Openness:** The gripper openness `g_t` is estimated by averaging the blue channel's low-response pixels across all views for a given time step.
*   **Reconstructing 3D Semantic Points:** For each semantic point (position, normal, up), a multi-view geometric procedure is used:
    1.  A 2D point is selected from the heatmap in the *main view* by weighted averaging, serving as a 2D anchor.
    2.  A ray is cast from the main-view camera center through this 2D anchor point.
    3.  A set of candidate 3D points are sampled along this ray.
    4.  Each candidate 3D point is projected into a *side view*, and the 3D point whose projection best matches the corresponding side-view heatmap response is selected as the reconstructed 3D point. This process resolves depth ambiguity.
*   **From Reconstructed Points to 7-DoF Action:** Once the three semantic 3D points (`q_pos_t`, `q_up_t`, `q_normal_t`) are reconstructed, the 7-DoF action is recovered:
    *   `p_t` is directly `q_pos_t`.
    *   `e_x` and `e_z` axes are derived from the normalized vectors between `q_up_t` and `q_pos_t`, and `q_pos_t` and `q_normal_t`, respectively. `e_y` is obtained via a cross product.
    *   The end-effector orientation `θ_t` is determined from these axes.
    *   The decoded gripper openness `g_t` is combined with `p_t` and `θ_t` to form the final 7-DoF action `a_t`.

The authors note that decoding error is primarily due to discretization (sampling interval, heatmap resolution), which can be improved by finer sampling or higher resolution.

#### 4.3. Training Unified World Action Model

A large pre-trained video generator (specifically Wan 2.2 [56]) is fine-tuned to jointly model multi-view robot videos and multi-view action videos.
*   **Multi-view Video-Action Tokenization and Packing:** For each camera view, an RGB observation clip `V^(v)` and an aligned action-frame clip `A^(v)` are encoded into the backbone's latent space using a 3D-VAE. These latent sequences are then concatenated temporally to form a single input sequence `X_v = [V^(v), A^(v)]`, creating a unified timeline of observation and action for each view. Multi-view data are processed with shared weights.
*   **Unified Training via Multiple Mask Strategies:** To enable a single model to perform multiple tasks, diverse mask strategies are applied to the concatenated latent sequence `X_v` within a diffusion-style denoising framework:
    1.  **Action & Video Joint Generation:** Masks both `V` and `A` tokens, except for the first observation frame, requiring the model to generate future video and action conditioned on text and camera inputs.
    2.  **Action-Conditioned Video Generation:** Keeps `A` tokens visible while masking `V`, training the model to synthesize visual observations consistent with provided actions.
    3.  **Video-to-Action Labeling:** Keeps `V` tokens visible while masking `A`, training the model to infer action images from the input video.
    4.  **Video-Only Generation:** For data without action annotations, only video tokens are used with the denoising objective.
*   **Camera Conditioning:** Camera Plücker embeddings are injected into the backbone to maintain multi-view consistency and enable camera-controlled generation.
*   **Optimization Objective:** The model is fine-tuned using a flow matching objective on the masked latent tokens. An L2 loss is minimized between the predicted and target velocities, conditioned on the input sequence, text prompt, and camera parameters.
*   **Training Datasets:** Training utilizes a mixture of:
    *   **RLBench [23]:** Simulated data providing accurate action and camera signals, augmented for visual diversity.
    *   **DROID [26]:** Real-robot data with comprehensive annotations, filtered for quality.
    *   **BridgeV2 [55]:** High-quality real-world videos, primarily used for video-only generation due to lacking intrinsic camera labels and action-camera alignment (estimated with VGGT [57]).

This comprehensive approach aims to create a robust, generalizable world action model capable of diverse robotic tasks.

### 5. Main Findings and Results

The experiments evaluated "Action Images" across several tasks in both simulation and real-world settings, primarily focusing on text-controlled action and video joint generation.

#### 5.1. Text-Controlled Action & Video Joint Generation

This was treated as the primary evaluation setting, assessing the model's ability to generate future robot videos and corresponding action images from language instructions and initial multi-view observations. The decoded action images yield executable controls. All evaluations in this section were multi-view, one-trial, open-loop, reflecting a challenging zero-shot setup.

*   **Zero-Shot Policy Results:**
    *   On **RLBench tasks** (where specific tasks were removed from training, but environment/robot were seen), "Action Images" achieved success rates of 30% for "pick cup," 60% for "reach target," 50% for "close drawer," and 15% for "close laptop." These results generally surpassed baselines such as MV-Policy, π0.5, MolmoAct, TesserAct, and Cosmos-Policy, which often showed 0-20% success rates on these tasks.
    *   On **Real-World tasks** (unseen objects, environments, and robot arm - xArm), "Action Images" demonstrated success rates of 40% for "Place Cup," 20% for "Pick Unseen Toy," 15% for "Pick Tissue," 45% for "Close Drawer," and 10% for "Close Box." Baselines typically exhibited 0-10% success rates.
    *   The results indicate that "Action Images" provided superior zero-shot performance, particularly under strong distribution shifts, supporting the claim that pixel-grounded action representation enhances generalizability.

*   **RLBench In-Domain Results:**
    *   On in-domain RLBench tasks, "Action Images" without an additional head achieved competitive performance, for example, 55% for "close box," 60% for "close door," and 60% for "close drawer," comparable to or slightly better than baselines like TesserAct (40% "close box," 25% "close door," 70% "close drawer") and Cosmos-Policy (40% "close box," 15% "close door," 80% "close drawer").
    *   When an **optional learned action head** (lightweight MLP) was added, performance significantly improved, reaching 80% for "close box," 65% for "close door," and 80% for "close drawer." This suggests the learned action image representation effectively supports enhanced action decoding when additional supervision is available.

*   **Joint Generation Quality:**
    *   The model's ability to accurately predict both future robot videos and corresponding actions was evaluated against world models (Cosmos-Predict, Cosmos-Policy, TesserAct).
    *   For **video quality** (PSNR, SSIM, FVD, LPIPS), "Action Images" consistently outperformed baselines. For example, it achieved a PSNR of 23.48 (compared to TesserAct's 20.83) and an SSIM of 78.62% (compared to TesserAct's 59.20%). FVD and LPIPS metrics also showed improvements, indicating better perceptual and temporal realism.
    *   For **action quality** (2D and 3D trajectory error), "Action Images" maintained accuracy, achieving a 2D Error of 1.61 and a 3D Error of 12.2 x 10^-3, which were competitive with or better than baselines (e.g., TesserAct's 2D Error 1.84, 3D Error 19.0 x 10^-3).

#### 5.2. Additional Unified-Model Capabilities

The research also evaluated the versatility of the unified model on other tasks.

*   **Action-Conditioned Video Generation:**
    *   When provided with an action sequence, the model generated future robot videos. Compared to Tora, a 2D trajectory-conditioned video generation baseline, "Action Images" achieved better results across all standard video metrics (PSNR 31.35 vs. 19.76, SSIM 67.16% vs. 52.43%, FVD 115.02 vs. 187.41, LPIPS 21.78% vs. 39.62%). This indicates effective utilization of action inputs for video prediction.

*   **Video-to-Action Labeling:**
    *   The model's capability to infer action-related motion directly from input videos was tested against point-tracking baselines, TAPIR and CoTracker3. "Action Images" outperformed both, achieving a trajectory error of 5.785 (compared to TAPIR's 14.80 and CoTracker's 12.91) and significantly higher Jaccard@4 and average Jaccard scores (64.92% vs. 40.26% for TAPIR; 46.71% vs. 29.77% for TAPIR average). This suggests the pixel-grounded action representation is effective for extracting action information from video.

#### 5.3. Qualitative Results

*   **Real-World Zero-Shot Rollouts (xArm):** The model demonstrated the ability to generalize to unseen real-world objects and environments (e.g., "Close the paper box"). Predicted tracking trajectories and 3D visualizations showed alignment with scene geometry, and the decoded trajectories were executable on the real robot, matching the generated motion.
*   **Zero-Shot Generation on FR3M Rooms:** On unseen objects, tasks, and environments (e.g., "Pick up the mouse" in FR3M rooms), the model produced videos with more accurate target localization and coherent action images, even for datasets (BridgeV2) where action supervision was not initially available, indicating transferability.

The findings collectively indicate that the proposed pixel-grounded action images enable a unified model to achieve improved zero-shot policy generalization, better video-action joint generation quality, and strong performance in related tasks like action-conditioned video generation and video-to-action labeling.

### 6. Significance and Potential Impact

The "Action Images" framework presents a significant development in robotics world modeling and policy learning by addressing the long-standing challenge of translating video generation capabilities into generalizable robot control. Its core contribution lies in conceptualizing robot control as a pixel-grounded visual prediction problem, thereby enabling a unified video-space representation of observation and action.

The key aspects of its significance and potential impact include:

*   **Enhanced Policy Generalization:** By representing 7-DoF robot actions as interpretable multi-view action images, the model can leverage the extensive pre-trained knowledge of video backbones more directly for control. This approach has demonstrated improved zero-shot policy performance in both simulated (RLBench) and real-world environments with unseen objects, tasks, and robot embodiments. This suggests a potential path towards more robust and adaptable robot systems that can operate in novel situations without extensive re-training.
*   **Unified World Model Architecture:** The framework integrates multiple robot learning tasks—video-action joint generation, action-conditioned video generation, and video-to-action labeling—within a single generative model. This unification simplifies model development and training, potentially reducing computational resources and enabling a more holistic understanding of robot dynamics and interaction.
*   **Reduced Reliance on Separate Modules:** By eliminating the need for a separate policy head or action module, the video backbone itself acts as the zero-shot policy. This design streamlines the architecture and aims to alleviate the generalization bottlenecks often associated with specialized control modules.
*   **Improved Action Interpretability and Debugging:** The pixel-grounded action images provide an interpretable visual representation of robot actions. This could facilitate better understanding, analysis, and debugging of robot behaviors compared to abstract low-dimensional action vectors. The multi-view nature also aids in reconstructing 3D actions, offering a clearer representation of spatial movements.
*   **Advancement in Data Efficiency and Transfer Learning:** By making actions native to the video space, the model can potentially benefit more effectively from large-scale, diverse video datasets, even those not originally annotated with precise robot actions. The capability to learn from a mixture of simulated and real-world data, including video-only generation, suggests greater flexibility in leveraging available data.
*   **Future Closed-Loop Control Potential:** While the current system primarily demonstrates strong open-loop results, the authors acknowledge the importance of closed-loop control. The advancements in efficient action prediction and generation provide a foundation for future work to distill the model for faster inference and integrate it into real-time feedback loops, which could lead to more practical and responsive robotic applications.

The identified limitations, primarily concerning the current open-loop nature of the system, highlight areas for future research, such as integrating diffusion acceleration and distillation techniques for faster inference and deployment in closed-loop control pipelines. Overall, "Action Images" offers a novel and promising direction for end-to-end policy learning, suggesting that grounding action in pixels is a viable route toward more generalizable and unified robotics world modeling.
overview:
## Bridging the Gap Between Video Prediction and Policy Generalization

In the pursuit of generalist robot policies, world models have emerged as a powerful tool. These models learn to predict the visual future of an environment, effectively "hallucinating" how a scene evolves in response to a robot's actions. However, a significant discrepancy remains: while contemporary video generation models can produce visually realistic future frames, they often struggle to translate this predictive ability into robust policy generalization, particularly when faced with unseen environments or novel robot embodiments. 

![Action Images Overview](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.06168v1/x1.png)
*Figure 1: Overview of the Action Images framework. It unifies robot observation and action into a common video space, enabling a pre-trained video backbone to serve as a zero-shot policy.*

A primary reason for this bottleneck is the way actions are traditionally represented within these models. Most existing methods treat actions as low-dimensional tokens or abstract latent vectors that are detached from the spatial context of the image. This detachment shifts the burden of generalization to a specialized control head or action module, which often fails to leverage the rich, spatially-grounded knowledge inherent in large-scale pre-trained video backbones. 

The "Action Images" framework addresses this by reformulating policy learning as a multiview video generation task. By representing robot actions as pixel-grounded images that explicitly track the motion of the robot arm in 3D space, the model makes actions "native" to the video space. This allows the video backbone itself to function as a zero-shot policy, directly generating actions as visual signals without the need for additional, non-spatial modules.

## Action Images: Grounding Control in Pixels

The core of this approach is the transformation of a 7-Degrees-of-Freedom (7-DoF) robot action into a visual format. A standard robot action at time $t$, denoted as $a_t = [p_t, \theta_t, g_t]$, consists of the end-effector's 3D position $p_t$, its orientation $\theta_t$, and a binary gripper state $g_t$ (open or closed). 

To make this action interpretable by a video model, it is first mapped to three semantic 3D points that capture the pose of the gripper:
1.  **Position Point**: $q_{\text{pos}_t} = p_t$, representing the center of the end-effector.
2.  **Up Point**: $q_{\text{up}_t} = p_t + \ell R(\theta_t)e_x$, representing a canonical in-plane direction.
3.  **Normal Point**: $q_{\text{normal}_t} = p_t + \ell R(\theta_t)(-e_z)$, representing the normal to the gripper plane.

In these equations, $R(\theta_t)$ is the rotation matrix derived from the orientation, and $\ell$ is a small constant length. These 3D points are then projected into the image space of each camera view $v$ using the camera's known intrinsic and extrinsic parameters. 

![Action Image Rendering](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.06168v1/x2.png)
*Figure 2: The rendering process of an Action Image. The position, normal, and up points are encoded into the Red, Green, and Blue channels of an RGB image as Gaussian heatmaps.*

The resulting 2D points are rendered into an "Action Image" $A^{(v)}_t \in \mathbb{R}^{H \times W \times 3}$ using Gaussian heatmaps across the three color channels:
*   **Red Channel**: Encodes the 2D projection of the position point.
*   **Green Channel**: Encodes the 2D projection of the normal point.
*   **Blue Channel**: Encodes the 2D projection of the up point. 

To handle the binary gripper state $g_t$ without adding extra channels, it is injected into the background of the blue channel (pixels where the Gaussian response is low). This creates a unified visual representation where action videos share the same spatial and temporal structure as the observation videos $O^{(v)}$.

## A Unified World Action Model Architecture

With actions represented as images, the framework employs a large pre-trained video generator—specifically Wan 2.2—to jointly model observation and action. The model treats both as sequences of latent tokens.

For each camera view $v$, the RGB observation clip $V^{(v)}$ and the aligned action-frame clip $A^{(v)}$ are encoded into a latent space using a 3D Variational Autoencoder (VAE). These sequences are concatenated temporally to form a single input sequence $X_v = [V^{(v)}, A^{(v)}]$, effectively creating a unified timeline for each view.

![Unified Training Strategy](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.06168v1/x4.png)
*Figure 3: The unified model architecture and masking strategies. Diverse masks allow the same backbone to perform joint generation, action-conditioned video prediction, and video-to-action labeling.*

To enable a single model to perform multiple robotic tasks, the authors utilize a variety of masking strategies during training:
1.  **Joint Video-Action Generation**: Both $V$ and $A$ tokens are masked (except for the initial frame), training the model to predict future scenes and the actions required to achieve them.
2.  **Action-Conditioned Video Generation**: Action tokens are kept visible while observation tokens are masked, training the model to synthesize video consistent with a given control sequence.
3.  **Video-to-Action Labeling**: Observation tokens are visible while action tokens are masked, training the model to infer the underlying actions from a video of a robot moving.

The model is optimized using a flow-matching objective, where it learns to predict the "velocity" of noise towards the target data, conditioned on text prompts and camera Plücker embeddings. This camera conditioning is crucial for maintaining consistency across multiple views.

## Decoding Actions from Visual Predictions

When the model generates an Action Image sequence, these visual signals must be converted back into physical 7-DoF robot commands to be executable. This decoding process leverages multi-view geometry to resolve the depth ambiguities inherent in 2D projections.

The decoding for a single time step follows these steps:
1.  **Gripper State**: The state $g_t$ is recovered by averaging the blue channel's background values across all views.
2.  **3D Point Reconstruction**: For each semantic point (position, normal, up), the model identifies a 2D anchor point in a "main" view heatmap. It then casts a ray from that camera's center through the anchor.
3.  **Cross-View Matching**: Candidate 3D points are sampled along this ray. Each candidate is projected into a "side" view, and the point that best matches the response in the side-view heatmap is selected as the reconstructed 3D point.
4.  **Pose Recovery**: Once the three 3D points ($q_{\text{pos}_t}$, $q_{\text{up}_t}$, $q_{\text{normal}_t}$) are reconstructed, the end-effector position $p_t$ and orientation $\theta_t$ are calculated by deriving the orthogonal axes $e_x$, $e_y$, and $e_z$ from the vectors between these points.

![Decoding Mechanism](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.06168v1/x3.png)
*Figure 4: The decoding process. Multi-view heatmaps are used via ray casting and back-projection to reconstruct the 3D semantic points required for robot control.*

## Experimental Performance and Generalization

The Action Images framework was evaluated on its ability to perform zero-shot tasks in both simulated and real-world environments. In the zero-shot setting, the model is tested on tasks, objects, or environments it did not see during training.

In **RLBench** simulations, Action Images significantly outperformed established baselines like π0.5 and Cosmos-Policy. For example, on the "pick cup" task, Action Images achieved a 30% success rate compared to the 0-10% seen in other models. On "close drawer," it reached a 50% success rate.

The model's generalization was further tested in the **real world** using an xArm robot. Even when facing entirely unseen objects and environments, the model demonstrated the ability to generate coherent video-action sequences. It achieved a 40% success rate on "Place Cup" and a 45% success rate on "Close Drawer" in a zero-shot manner. These results suggest that grounding action in pixels allows the model to transfer the visual-spatial common sense learned from massive video datasets to the domain of robotic control.

![Real-World Zero-Shot Performance](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.06168v1/x5.png)
*Figure 5: Qualitative results of zero-shot real-world rollouts. The model accurately predicts the motion trajectories and executes the "Close the paper box" task on an unseen robot arm.*

Beyond policy execution, the unified model showed superior performance in auxiliary tasks. In **action-conditioned video generation**, it outperformed specialized models like Tora in video quality metrics (PSNR, SSIM, FVD). In **video-to-action labeling**, it surpassed point-tracking baselines like CoTracker3, demonstrating a more robust understanding of the robot's physical movement within a scene.

## Significance and Future Directions

The significance of this work lies in its unification of action and perception. By treating robot control as just another set of pixels to be predicted, it removes the need for complex, task-specific architectures that often fail to generalize. The Action Images representation provides an interpretable, spatially-grounded bridge between high-level video generation and low-level physical control.

Currently, the system primarily operates in an open-loop fashion, generating a sequence of actions based on initial observations. A key area for future development is the transition to high-frequency closed-loop control. This will likely involve techniques like model distillation or diffusion acceleration to reduce inference latency, allowing the robot to react dynamically to changes in the environment in real-time. 

By demonstrating that pixel-grounded actions can inherit the strong generalization of large video models, the framework provides a promising path toward truly general-purpose robotic agents capable of operating in the complexity of the real world.

keyInsights: Grounding robot actions directly in the pixel space as visual signals allows large video backbones to inherently leverage their extensive visual knowledge for action reasoning and control.

Representing actions as multi-view images resolves 3D ambiguities and integrates action directly into the visual prediction problem, enabling the video backbone itself to function as a zero-shot policy without separate modules.

A unified video-space representation of observations and actions enables a single generative model to perform diverse robot learning tasks, simplifying architecture and training across different objectives.
originalProblem: Existing robotics world models struggle to translate strong video generation capabilities into robust policy generalization, especially in novel environments.

Current action representations are often detached from visual space or rely on separate policy heads, limiting the direct leverage of rich, pre-trained video knowledge for control and hindering transferability.
Many generalist robot policy models face challenges with zero-shot transfer to entirely new environments, often failing to adapt beyond training scenes.
results: Achieved superior zero-shot policy success rates on unseen tasks, for example, up to 60% for 'reach target' on RLBench and 45% for 'close drawer' on real-world tasks, outperforming baselines which often showed 0-20% success.

Demonstrated improved video-action joint generation quality, with 'Action Images' outperforming baselines like TesserAct in video quality (PSNR 23.48 vs 20.83, SSIM 78.62% vs 59.20%) and maintaining competitive action quality.

Outperformed specialized baselines in action-conditioned video generation (e.g., PSNR 31.35 vs. Tora's 19.76) and video-to-action labeling (trajectory error 5.785 vs. TAPIR's 14.80), showcasing the model's versatility.
solution: Robot 7-DoF actions (position, orientation, gripper) are converted into multi-view pixel-grounded 'action images' by projecting semantic 3D points (end-effector position, up, normal) into 2D Gaussian heatmaps across multiple camera views.
A large pre-trained video generator is fine-tuned to jointly model multi-view robot observation videos and the newly created multi-view action videos within a unified video-space.

Diverse masking strategies are employed during training to enable a single model to perform multiple tasks, including video-action joint generation, action-conditioned video generation, and video-to-action labeling.
summary.summary: Action Images reformulates robot policy learning as multiview video generation, converting 7-DoF robot actions into pixel-grounded images. This approach enables a unified world model to achieve superior zero-shot policy generalization on unseen tasks and environments, alongside improved video-action joint generation quality.

title: Action Images: End-to-End Policy Learning via Multiview Video Generation
